mappings.rationaleAI: The complete biological structure organized hierarchically from cells to organ systems (D, E), covering circulatory, digestive, endocrine, immune, musculoskeletal, nervous, reproductive, and respiratory systems.
The circulatory system serves cell metabolism through transport of oxygen, nutrients, and waste (E.1.b), operating as pulmonary and systemic circuits.
Skin as a tissue for assimilation and protection (D.2), with classification across taxa and reproductive and other tissue types (D.5).
Bone as mineralized connective tissue providing support and movement (D.4), with cortical and cancellous layers performing mechanical, hematopoietic, and homeostatic functions.
Hormones regulate coordination through endocrine tissues (D.3) and plant hormones including auxin distributed spatially (B.2), connecting vertebrate and plant physiology.
Blood as a specialized connective tissue for transport (D.2) and hemopoietic tissues producing blood cells (D.5).
Lungs as respiratory tissue for gas exchange (D.2) within the respiratory system serving cell metabolism (E.1.b).
The brain as nervous tissue for coordination (D.3) within the nervous system relating to the environment (E.1.a).
Skeletal muscle as tissue for support and movement (D.4) within the muscular system relating to the environment (E.1.a).
The skeleton as structural framework with bone, cartilage, and connective tissue (D.4) within the skeletal system (E.1.a).
The eye as a sensory organ with nervous and sensory tissues for coordination (D.3), converting light to neural signals.
Sensory reception involves nervous and sensory tissues for coordination (D.3), with specialized receptor cells transducing physical signals.
Flowers as reproductive organs with diverse varieties of reproductive organs and organ systems in vascular plants (C.3.c).
Physiology studies biological form and function (A), physiology of organs in vascular plants (C.2), and interrelationships between organ systems including feedback mechanisms (E.2).
Anatomy studies structure of organisms and their parts, relevant to biological form and function (A), animal tissues (D), and animal organ systems (E).
Digestive tissues for assimilation and transport (D.2) within the digestive system serving cell metabolism (E.1.b).
Photosynthesis occurs in well-differentiated vascular plant tissues (B.2), including chlorophyll-bearing cells within the fundamental ground tissues.
Trees exhibit well-differentiated tissues in vascular plants (B.2) with diverse sizes and forms of organ systems showing unlimited growth potential (C.3).
Fruits are seed-bearing structures of angiosperms, representing varieties of reproductive organs in vascular plants (C.3.c).
Algae as nonvascular plants have relatively undifferentiated tissues (B.1), ranging from unicellular to multicellular forms.
The nervous system comprises nervous and sensory tissues (D.3) as a specialized organ system relating to the environment (E.1.a).
Arthropods have chitinous exoskeletons as support tissues (D.4) with open circulatory and nervous organ systems (E).
The heart as cardiac muscle tissue (D.4) pumping within the circulatory system serving cell metabolism (E.1.b).
Immune tissues for defense (D.2) demonstrating functional interdependence with other organ systems through feedback mechanisms (E.2).
The ear as a sensory organ for hearing and balance, comprising nervous and sensory tissues (D.3).
Seeds as reproductive units of spermatophytes relate to varieties of reproductive organs in vascular plants (C.3.c).
Sex and gamete production relate to the reproductive system serving genetic continuity (E.1.c).
Eggs as organic reproductive vessels relate to the reproductive system serving genetic continuity (E.1.c).
The liver as a metabolic hub tissue for assimilation, storage, and transport (D.2) within the digestive/excretory system serving cell metabolism (E.1.b).
Molluscs possess distinctive tissue organization including open circulatory systems with haemocoel (D) and organ systems spanning nervous, digestive, and reproductive functions (E).
Zoology covers the structure, classification, and physiology of animals, directly relevant to animal tissues (D) and animal organ systems (E).
